Aliens Never Invented Democracy
content:

As soon as Kang was elected President of the United States, he made some changes.
Atvar summed up the Race’s view of the United States in one scornful word: “Snoutcounters! How do they have the hubris to imagine they can build a land that amounts to anything by counting one another’s snouts?”
— Worldwar: In the Balance

A Sub-Trope of Humans Are Special and Humans Are Diplomats, in some works of fiction it seems that Humanity is the only civilization in the Universe that ever thought it was a good idea to choose leaders by voting.

Sometimes this is done because the writer wants to make the aliens truly alien in culture, thus having weird, even sometimes incomprehensible, political systems is a way. Due to Hollywood and Creators Culture Carryovers, an absolute monarchy or dictatorship is foreign to most western pop culture, while not having to come up with truly non-human systems since Most Writers Are Human. In some works it could be a way to present humanity's nice democratic system vs the aliens' evil Empire, which is very common when The Federation faces The Empire. (This is especially common in works made during the Cold War for obvious reasons.) Sometimes is just a matter of Planet of Hats: some writers just can't think that a Proud Warrior Race can be democratic (despite the fact that we have many examples in our own world) because, well warriors are not democrats, also in scenarios of Alien Invasion, having the invaders enjoying a democracy would feel kind of contradictory (despite, again, many examples in our history where democratic republics held vast, cruel empires and subjugated other peoples without considering this a contradiction).

Compare with Aliens Never Invented the Wheel for the technical counterpart. See also Feudal Future and Privately Owned Society for when the humans don't have democracy any more either. Often overlaps with One-Federation Limit, with humans as the one democratic government.

Feedback
Video Example(s):
Simpsons 1996 Election
Share Video

Treehouse of Horror VII includes an election in which aliens kidnap and impersonate candidates President Bill Clinton and Senator Bob Dole. And though unfamiliar with democratic campaigning, they quickly get the hang of it.

content:

in: Governments, Galactic superpowers, Universes
Confederation of Earth
SAVE
EDIT
Alternate timeline
(covers information from Confederation of Earth)

Confederation of Earth

Confederation flag standard (2401)

Founded:

2152

Status:

Active

Government:

Autocratic Oligarchy

Military:

Confederation Corps (Army based stellar fleet)

Homeworld:

Earth

Capital:

San Francisco

Species:

Human (soley)

Central Leaders:

Annika Hansen

Commanding Officers:

Jean-Luc Picard
Benjamin Sisko

Currency:

Confederacy Latinum credit

 

For an alternate timeline account of this Organization Click Here

The Confederation of Earth is an Authoritarian Oligarchic interstellar nation-state that is a successful amalgamation of several fascistic groups on Earth from the late 21st century. Its central goal is to create and racially homogenous and militarily secure Milky Way Galaxy for a now more socially introverted and Xenophobic Humanity, centrally by eradicating all alien species residing there.

It was created by manipulations in history from Q in 2024 to create another morality test for Jean-Luc Picard in 2401. However, the State's technical power grew so large, and Q, now a much older being in a deteriative state, accidentally transported other ships from Other universes and timelines along with Picard.

The Confederation ended up trying to make incursions into other realities through gaps in reality accidentally created by Q including creating a centuries long war with another Starfleet variant, the United Space Force.

These conflicts garnered the attention of the Federation, Imperial Federation, 2250s era Section 31, and several alien species, leading to near full scale war against the Confederation. This event was later named as the first "Multiversal Crisis" as the aftermath of this event would lead to several more similar events throughout the 25th century.

The Confederation is considered the "mirrored" (morally opposite) universal government of the United Space Force, as the Terran Empire is the moral opposite of Starfleet.

Contents
1 System of Government
2 Military Structure
3 History
4 External Links
System of Government

The Confederation, while centrally run by a Presidential based central pseudo-parliamentary republic structure. In reality, this is a political smokescreen for the actual ruling forces, which are a group of Earth and Offworld colony based super corporations that control and run all of Earth's technical and energy infrastructure, such as Yoyodyne Industries.

The publically shown government is headed by a central President, holding as Chairperson over a Council of "Magistrates". These Magistrates work similar to 20th century representative government. Each one speaking for the planetary regions or colonies of each regional sector.

However moreso in reality the government's task is to reinforce the xenophobic and intellectually backward tendencies of Human society to continue justifying the military's actions against all alien and outer universal factions. And to continue to give the Planets corporations massive profits through planetary annexation even though they live in a post scarcity technical society which these demands should be meaningless, but having a dominant hand over the planets resources does give them absolute political control.

Below that while it seems like the rest of Human society goes without, in reality due to having so many rich resources no one really goes hungry or homeless. However, Society within this universe has become so self serving that they are willing to participate in this corrupt system as they receive nearly as many perks as the elite (although not as elegant or high end) such as financial and resource bribes for smaller companies, gifted alien slave workforces, and guarantees on resource claims through piracy. Humanity gladly joins in on the corruption, as long as they are guaranteed something.

Military Structure
History

In 2024 years after the overthrow of Khan Noonien Singh and the end of the Eugenics Wars, another geneticist that was a survivor of the wars Adam Soong was in secret trying to create another project based on genetic superiority.

While in its infancy, synthetic engineered human technology or "Replicant" technology was attempted by several corporations, including the Tyrell Corporation in Los Angeles in 2019. Most projects were shut down due to hard laws against attempting to create another crisis of beings that believed they were superior to basic Humanity.

Adam, however, was attempting to mass produce such beings secretly, believing they could assist Humanity in the future. However, they were dying due to a genetic flaw. Each one living for only a few hours before complete organ failure. Adam was also in competition with Picard's 3xGreat Grandmother Renee for the development of an engineered airborne microorganism that could eat large amounts of artificial carbons in Earths atmosphere. Possibly reducing the climate crisis of general pollution and nuclear radiation leftover from the global conflicts of decades earlier.

Adam knew if Renee beat him out, he would be out of possible billions in genetic research.

It was because of this Q went to this particular time period and offered Adam a deal. Remove Renee from her discovery, and Q in return would give him the solution for the genetic defect and allow him to become the success Adam believed himself to be.

External Links
Confederation of Earth article at Memory Alpha, the canon Star Trek wiki.
Confederation of Earth article at Memory Beta, the non-canon Star Trek wiki.

title:
Stack Exchange
scifi.stackexchange.com › questions › 64343 › alien-universe-is-there-any-information
content:
Alien universe - is there any information about the political situation on earth? As I know, in first three films, there were any information about wars or global government on earth or in outer space, but many times I heard that the Company wanted xenomorphs for the purpose of using them as a biological weapon.
Are you only referring to the films or are you happy to get info from the books/comics, etc. Many of those are set on Earth.Valorum–Valorum2014-07-29 21:11:02 +00:00CommentedJul 29, 2014 at 21:11
Do you count Prometheus? The timeline on the official website atweylandindustries.com/timelineare indicates that the US govt still exists but contracts out a lot of functions (like the Colonial Marines) to the Weyland Corporation.Hypnosifl–Hypnosifl2014-07-30 03:04:13 +00:00CommentedJul 30, 2014 at 3:04
2No one should ever countPrometheus.James Sheridan–James Sheridan2014-07-30 08:47:51 +00:00CommentedJul 30, 2014 at 8:47
Yup:companies are evil.Paul D. Waite–Paul D. Waite2017-03-12 22:16:14 +00:00CommentedMar 12, 2017 at 22:16
InAliens, the Haven space station was orbiting Earth, and there is one scene in the film where it can be seen outside the station. There is no obvious damage to the planet in that shot. That, unfortunately, is the closest that any of the films get to a discussion of Earth, prior toAlien: Resurrection. Many of the books and comics deal with Earth.
I'll mainly useXenopediaas a source, which for the most part cites primary sources.This blog entryis also a good collection of information we have about the political situation.
Politically, the nations of Earth seem to have merged or united into different "empires". The one called theThree World Empireconsists of Japan and the UK (which the merger of the British Weyland Industries/Corporation and the Japanese Yutani Corporation is either the result of, or played a large part in), as well as several developing countries. This empire seems to also be part of, or allied with, the United Americas somehow, although this has not been fleshed out.
North America, Central America and South America have merged (obstensibly as a response to the Three World Empire) and created a political entity called theUnited Americas. It doesn't seem to be a country, but a union of sovereign states (more akin to today's European Union), where the most powerful one seems to be the United States because of their position as a starfaring, colonizing power, and they also control theUnited States Colonial Marine Corps(USCM).
Private companies also have significant political pull, and are implied to control governments. AmArc is the name of one such company, which we don't know much about. "The Company", Weyland-Yutani, is obviously also very powerful on Earth. As they own several colonies, they often use the USCM for campaigns concerning their real estate in outer space, which probably explains how they want to use the Aliens as biological weapons, especially combined with theWashington Treatywhich seemingly allows the United Americas to carry out incursions on colony worlds to assert their power (as was done at the colonyCristóbalto quell a strike and remove a leader).
Apparently, in 2600 AD (which is several centuries after the movies take place) Weyland-Yutani even bought the USCM (Alien: Invasion).

title:
Quora
quora.com › Why-are-alien-planets-or-civilizations-always-considered-to-have-a-monarch-type-of
content:
Skip to content
Skip to search
Sign In
Government
Science Fiction (genre)
Movies and TV Shows
Intelligent Alien Civiliz...
Monarchies
Extraterrestrial Life
Government Systems
Science Fiction Movies
Alien Cultures
+ 5
Why are alien planets or civilizations always considered to have a monarch type of government in movies or series?
All related (37)
Sort
Recommended
Planet UFO
Answered by 
James H. Jenkins
Author has 3.3K answers and 4.5M answer views
 · 
Jun 20, 2021

Simple answer: it costs less in sets, actor pay, and extra pay along with craft tables for Captain Kirk to meet the one leader or Three person high council than to speak in front of the UN, and the combined governments of 170 countries. Writers do this with novels also. It is less confusing. It is a trope of convenience.

If you wrote a page each on the trip to each government, and only a page, that’s the whole book, nothing else happens.

So:
Ship appears in orbit,

Beam down, or take shuttle.

Meet the leaders, discover the problem.

Challenged as the leaders resist.

Mr. Spock’s home planet, Vulcan, is governed by a council of elders, not a monarch. Superman’s home planet, Krypton, was a planet ruled by its top scientists and technologists (until they goofed and blew it up).

What’s pretty consistent about alien civilizations isn’t that they’re all monarchies, but that they haven’t split up into umpteen countries, cultures, and languages like the people of Earth have. I think there’s at least one exception to that too, but my memory is hazy on it.

When you have to scope your invented world to a novel, film, or short story, you simply don’t have time to go int

Why are aliens in fiction always designed as humanoid?

Studied Aerospace and Aeronautical Engineering at University of Illinois at Urbana-ChampaignAuthor has 7.4K answers and 108.9M answer views
 · 
6mo

That’s mostly true for on-screen aliens, especially before 2000s when CGI was difficult and unrealistic. You have to depict them using human actors in costumes and makeups. Legacy franchises (like Star Trek and Star Wars) must continue with what was set up a long time ago.

However, this is so ingrained in our culture that even with CGI we still prefer humanoid aliens. Part of it is just familiarity; you can “read” a humanoid better than a, say, 6-armed squid monster or a jellyfish.

With humanoids, you can get away with less “worldbuilding” and explanations because you can base it on people. How

Three overlapping storytelling, production and cultural reasons explain why alien worlds in movies and TV so often use monarch-like governments.

Instant recognizability and shorthand
Monarchies are visually and narratively compact: a throne, crown, palace, royal ceremony instantly convey "this is a centralized, hierarchical society" without exposition.
Visual shorthand matters in film/TV where screen time is limited; a monarch gives audiences immediate context for power relationships and stakes.
Dramatic clarity and conflict
A single ruler creates an obvious target for plots: assassination, success

Well, it’s fiction.

One reason is that you want to keep the setting easily understandable. As the saying goes, “less is more”. You don’t need to understand the laws regulating grain trade to enjoy a political thriller. Too much details that are irrelevant to the plot is distracting, at best. With too many countries per planet, it will just be confusing.

Another reason is they want to give planets an identity. This is also why we often see those one-biome planets (like all of the planet is a giant desert).

Courtney Seligman
Emeritus Professor of Astronomy, Author (ErindalePublishing)Author has 2.5K answers and 1.3M answer views
 ·

Because in real life, there are enough different civilizations and power-hungry would-be leaders that there is no current way to combine them. The same thing is probably true of many, if not most, other planets with civilizations, but depicting that sort of situation in a series of movies or TV shows is far more complicated than having a single society, and would cost a lot more money and time, and producers usually don’t want to take the risks involved. In almost every sci-fi story incorporating aliens, the Earth is shown as a single government entity rather than a multitude of nations as we are currently. Is this the ultimate governance structure of the future?

Brian Blose
Software Developer, Novelist, Quora AddictAuthor has 1.8K answers and 5.2M answer views
 · 
10y

The single world government portrayed in science fiction is a hack to avoid writing about complex geopolitics. Would you really want to read hundreds of pages about the reactions of 196 countries to first contact with aliens? Even if you only include the major players, you would have the USA, China, Russia, the European Union (assuming they pool their resources to have more clout), and then state-funded terrorists and religious extremists who have particular opinions on the matter. A single government cuts away a lot of the politicking so that the story can focus on what everyone wants to r

There are a few reasons I can think of why authors often use this structure:

It is simply easier to tell the story of humans against - or in cooperation with - aliens if humans have a single world government.
The belief that alien contact and the (sadly likely impossible) ability to travel between stars in a timely fashion would have this effect on humanity - of realising that compared to these other species / planets, there is more that unites earth / humanity than divides us.
There has, over time, been a clear direction of travel to larger and larger entities, to use terminology borrowed fro

A single, planet-wide government is a common sci‑fi trope because it simplifies story logistics and dramatizes unified human response. But political, technological and social dynamics suggest several more plausible long-term governance architectures rather than an inevitable single global state. Below are the key drivers, plausible outcomes, and likely stability of each scenario.

Why the single-state trope is appealing (and misleading)

Narrative convenience: One polity equals one decision-maker for moral dilemmas, invasions, alliances and treaties.
Symbolic unity: Planetary government dramatizes

Studying Linguistics

Which government is in question as there is not a single government in charge of Humanity. Diffrent places on Earth would react in different ways yet this question is more of how would humanity react. Most governments would do their best to protect the country and their leaders yet there would be not much that they could do except sending out their own armies to protect their land. Yet the invasion would definitely be far more cultural than the movies, due to the diverse possibilities of alien life there is no reasonable way for us to predict what would happen.

content:

Why is it that so many Sci-Fi planets are ruled over by a single world government? Are there any works where a galactic power is still divided on its home-world?
Submission accepted by 
Corwyn B
Answer
Request
Follow
·
3
4 Answers
Sort
Recommended
James H. Jenkins
INFJ, Sci-Fi Writer/Cartographer, Traveller SFRPG Referee
 · 
Updated 4y

This pretty much started with Star Trek.

Although before Star Trek there were monolithic governments in science fiction novels.

The main idea here is that the heroic ship of plucky adventurers needs to go to a different world week-to-week.

It fits better for half hour or hour TV if the heroes can meet with one individual or a triumvirate Council of perhaps three individuals that run the planet.

It's much easier (and lazy, for certain) to write (or film) a story where heroes go to a desert world or forest world with one terrain type, and deal with the High Council or the President or the Emperor ra

Continue Reading
16
Aaron Davis
Space opera and military science fiction geek
 · 
Updated 5y

Several.

Just off the top of my head…

Star Wars Legends

In X-Wing #9: Starfighters of Adumar by Aaron Allston, the New Republic and the Empire (reunified by Daala and Pellaeon two years earlier in the novel Darksaber—no relation to the one in Clone Wars and Mandalorian, thank the Force) are both trying to get the planet Adumar to join them (its industrial output is cited as a motivating factor). Adumar, as it turns out, is involved in a cold war between two national blocs. The larger of the two, with whom both factions are initially negotiating, is led by the monarchy of Cartann, whose perator at

Continue Reading
5
John Biles
Historian (2005–present)
 · 
5y

Because the gradual consolidation of nations over the course of history seems likely to continue on worlds which don’t blow themselves to hell.

But also, it’s easier than having to create a bunch of nations, most of which won’t matter for the plot.

2
Lacey White
Studied at California (state)
 · 
5y

Most of it is plot convenience. It is hard enough to follow an spic story without having to involve all the various governments. You go to a world and find the “person of power.” Unless your story involves rebellion then you can have another group. But the more you have the harder it is for your reader to keep up with everyone.

title:
ScreenRant
screenrant.com › home › books › 10 sci-fi books with flawless worldbuilding
10 Sci-Fi Boo
content:
Memory Ngulube joined Screen Rant in April 2024 as part of the Movies and TV team. Since then, she has written for the Perennials and Streaming team, covering a wide range of topics from new releases to old movies and TV shows. Memory is currently a Classic TV writer.Before joining Screen Rant, Memory was a freelance copywriter who specialized in email marketing and social media management. While she enjoyed her job, her true passion was always movies and TV.When she isn’t writing for Screen Rant, Memory can be found reading, baking (which is one of her passions), or watching her favorite K-drama.
Great science fictionbooksdon’t just tell a story; they build a believable and fascinating world that readers can easily step immerse themselves in. When creating a universe, every detail must be carefully brought to life, without one being sacrificed for the other. As much as character development is an integral part of any story, it is nothing if the very foundation on which the premise is built is shaky.
While it's easy to believe that scale is equal to a flawless world, that isn’t always the case. There are somescience fiction novelswith a massive scope that aren’t able to make readers understand how their universe actually works. The sci-fi books with perfect worldbuilding ensure imagination is matched by structure, and every detail feels like part of a system rather than decoration. Fromnovels grounded in hard scienceto those that introduce new concepts, the world in these books feels alive because they behave like real ones.
Hyperion by Dan Simmons
The winner of the 1990 Hugo Award for best novel,Hyperionby Dan Simmons, tells an intricate tale that transcends space and time. Set in a distant future where humanity has spread across hundreds of planets after the destruction of Earth, the literary work is centered on seven strangers. These strangers were chosen for a final pilgrimage to the remote and dangerous world of Hyperion, a planet shrouded in mystery and home to the Time Tombs, ancient structures that move backward through time, and the terrifying Shrike, a nearly godlike being associated with pain, death, and prophecy.
Simmons does something extremely smart inHyperion. The novel is vast and has a lot of moving parts, which readers need to acclimatize to. Instead of overwhelming the reader with information,Hyperionunfolds through the personal narratives of the pilgrims, each exploring a different corner of the universe. Through a priest’s horror-filled experience, a soldier’s time-twisted war story, and a detective’s encounter with artificial intelligence, the reader gradually pieces together how this universe works.
The Left Hand Of Darkness by Ursula K. Le Guin
WithThe Left Hand of Darkness, Ursula K. Le Guin achieved something that various authors can only dream of;she won both the Hugo and Nebula awards. In the exceptional sci-fi novel, Genly Ai, an envoy from Ekumen, a vast interstellar alliance, is sent to Gethen, where he has been assigned to persuade its people to join a wider community of worlds. However, Genly quickly realizes that his biggest challenge isn’t politics or technology; it's Gethen's society, which operates on a different level than his own.
The Left Hand of Darknessis considered a groundbreaking novel because the people of Gethen are ambisexual, a concept which was rarely talked about during the time it was published.
At first glance,The Left Hand of Darknessis a simple story about alliances, but it's way more than that. As much as the icy landscapes and the incredible details about the planet that Le Guin includes in the novel are why it's so compelling, it's how the author builds a fully functioning society that sticks with readers. Karhide, for instance, is an absolutist monarchy while Orgoreyn is a Stalinist country. All this results in a world that is rich in culture and politics.
Foundation by Isaac Asimov
Isaac Asimov is widely considered one of the pioneers of science fiction, withFoundationproving why that's the case. Like many stories in science fiction, the novel is set in the future, where humanity has spread across the entire Milky Way and formed a vast Galactic Empire. The book begins when the Empire is at its most powerful, but also quietly decaying. Hari Seldon, a mathematician, develops a revolutionary science called psychohistory, which combines history, sociology, and statistical mathematics to predict the future behavior of large populations.
Using this science, Seldon foresees that the Galactic Empire is about to collapse into a long dark age lasting thousands of years. WithFoundationbeing set across the entire Milky Way, the novel gives a sense of a fully populated galaxy with thousands of worlds, cultures, and political systems. Because Asimov’s masterpiece takes place over centuries, readers truly understand the novel's scale and witness the rise and fall of multiple societies.
The Three-Body Problem by Liu Cixin
The Three-Body Problemis probably one of the most relevant hard science fiction novels of our time, as it gives a glance into how society would react to a looming invasion by extraterrestrial beings. The story starts with Ye Wenjie, a scientist who contacts an alien species called the Trisolarans, beings who live on a planet in a three-star system. Because the gravitational interactions between three suns are chaotic and unpredictable, their planet undergoes constant cycles of extreme heat and cold. As their civilization is unstable, they are desperate for a new home, and thanks to Ye Wenjie, they choose Earth.
Liu Cixin does an incredible job of building two worlds: Earth and Trisolaris. In Trisolaris, the author gives scientifically accurate details about how a planet with three suns would function while also showing the different reactions that humans would have to impending doom. The fact that the alien invasion isn’t immediate and spans centuries allowed Cixin to outline the different ways that the world evolves, adding a vastness toThe Three-Body Problem.
The Culture Series by Iain M. Banks
Iain M. Banks’The Cultureseries comprises 10 novels, but because of how the books are structured, they could be read in any order. Set in a far-future, post-scarcity civilization called the Culture, where advanced technology has eliminated poverty, disease, and most material limitations, humans and other species live freely and pursue pleasure.
InThe Cultureseries, Banks has created one of the most fully realized and logically consistent futuristic civilizations in science fiction. Every aspect of life in the novel flows naturally from its core premise of post-scarcity abundance to AI governance. Reading about an essentially perfect society might sound boring, butThe Cultureseries is the opposite of that, as it depicts a society that shifts toward questions of identity and personal freedom.
Dune by Frank Herbert
Frank Herbert’sDuneseries is often cited as one of the best works in the genre because of its detailed world-building. Paul Atreides, the young heir of House Atreides, whose family is given control of the desert planet Arrakis, the only source of spice, the most valuable substance in the universe. House Atreides, however, is betrayed, and Paul is forced to flee into the desert, where he eventually finds refuge among the Fremen.
The title of Herbert’s book itself is a direct reference to Arrakis, which, in a way, gives readers a glimpse into the novel’s worldbuilding and story. Arrakis isn’t merely a backdrop inDunebut a living ecological system with its sandworms, spice production, and water scarcity being as compelling as the political struggles.
The Dispossessed by Ursula K. Le Guin
Ursula K. Le Guin struck gold again withThe Dispossessed, a novel that won both the Hugo and Nebula awards in 1975 and 1974, respectively. The book takes place in two worlds: Anarres and Urras. Anarres is a harsh and barren moon where society is organized around anarchist principles. There is no government,
summary: When creating a universe, every detail must be carefully brought to life, without one being sacrificed for the other. At first glance,The Left Hand of Darknessis a simple story about alliances, but it's way more than that. Like many stories in science fiction, the novel is set in the future, where humanity has spread across the entire Milky Way and formed a vast Galactic Empire. The book begins when the Empire is at its most powerful, but also quietly decaying. Every aspect of life in the novel flows naturally from its core premise of post-scarcity abundance to AI governance.
